meta.description: Compiled real-world procedural insights for RestoKit. Flat tagged structure for flexible reuse across core/ files. Focuses on durable field logic, decision frameworks, safety patterns, equipment logic, and operator heuristics. Individual product specs, exact model performance data, and transient information are looked up on the fly by the voice AI.
meta.structure_note: Flat tagged array. One insight can belong to multiple tags. New optional fields support linkage to core/, decision logic, and voice-mode rendering. Product specs and model-specific data are explicitly out of scope.

scope.in_kit: Procedural logic, decision rules, heuristics, safety patterns, equipment logic (how/when/why to use), psychrometric targets, material response, containment strategy, demo strategy, contents handling.
scope.lookup_on_fly: Individual product specs, exact CFM/amps/weight of specific models, current manuals, part numbers, pricing, and jurisdiction-specific code details that change frequently.

insights.title: Hidden COL Investigation
insights.text: COL (Cause of Loss) is frequently hidden behind walls, ceilings, or under floors. Do not assume visible water intrusion from outside. Use moisture meter systematically to hunt for the source — this is often a 'Scooby-Doo' style investigation.
insights.conditions: Any water loss where source is not immediately obvious

voice_notes.tech: Hunt the source. Visible water is often not the origin. Meter systematically behind finishes.
voice_notes.deep: Many losses originate internally or travel through cavities. Assume nothing from exterior appearance alone.
voice_notes.smartass: The water didn't teleport in. Go find where it's actually coming from.
voice_notes.rookie: Don't just look at the wet spot. Check behind walls and under floors too.

insights.title: Internal Water Sources
insights.text: Water intrusion is often internal (broken pipe, appliance failure, toilet supply line, etc.) rather than external. Do not default to assuming the water came from outside the structure or from weather events.
insights.conditions: Most residential and light commercial water losses

voice_notes.tech: Internal sources are more common than people think. Check appliances, supply lines, and drains first.
voice_notes.deep: Weather-related claims are often misidentified. Internal plumbing failures are frequent and create different contamination profiles.
voice_notes.smartass: Half the time it's not the roof. It's the toilet supply line or the fridge ice maker.
voice_notes.rookie: Ask about recent plumbing work or appliance issues before assuming storm damage.

insights.title: Fast Slow Leak Knowledge
insights.text: Typically you know whether it is a fast or slow leak problem before leaving for the job based on the customer interview and initial description.
insights.conditions: Pre-job intake and dispatch

voice_notes.tech: You usually know fast vs slow leak from the call. Fast leaks mean more aggressive extraction and containment planning.
voice_notes.deep: Fast leaks create different Category/Class dynamics and require faster mobilization of high-capacity extraction.
voice_notes.smartass: If they say 'it's been leaking for a week' it's probably not a fast leak anymore.
voice_notes.rookie: Listen to how the customer describes it. Fast vs slow changes how you prep.

insights.title: Lead Asbestos Testing
insights.text: Initial assessment requires lead and asbestos testing if necessary (varies per state and home year). Testing must be completed before any demo starts. However, you can safely remove baseboards and outlet covers if the drywall is in good enough shape.
insights.conditions: Homes built before 1980 or when suspect materials are present

voice_notes.tech: Test for lead and asbestos before major demo. You can usually pull baseboards and outlet covers safely if drywall is intact.
voice_notes.deep: Regulatory requirements vary by state. Default to testing on older homes before disturbing suspect materials.
voice_notes.smartass: Don't turn a water job into a hazmat job by skipping the test.
voice_notes.rookie: On older houses, get the lead/asbestos test done before you start ripping things out.

insights.title: No Grout Pins
insights.text: Do not stick moisture meter pins into grout lines — it damages the material. Use pinless mode on the tile surface instead and compare readings to known dry areas.
insights.conditions: Tile and grout surfaces

voice_notes.tech: Stay in pinless mode on tile. Pinning grout lines damages the finish.
voice_notes.deep: Grout is porous and easily damaged. Non-invasive readings + comparison to known dry areas is more reliable.
voice_notes.smartass: Pinning grout is a great way to create new damage you get to explain later.
voice_notes.rookie: Use the pinless setting on tile. Don't poke holes in the grout.

insights.title: Fiber Cement Readings
insights.text: You have to be careful with tile readings. It is common for fiber cement board to be used with many screws which can read as wet with a non-invasive meter.
insights.conditions: Tile over fiber cement board substrates

voice_notes.tech: Fiber cement board with screws can false-positive as wet on pinless meters. Verify with multiple spots and known dry areas.
voice_notes.deep: Metal fasteners create localized high readings. Cross-reference with actual material condition and multiple measurement points.
voice_notes.smartass: Those screws will lie to your meter every time. Don't trust a single reading.
voice_notes.rookie: If it reads wet everywhere on tile, check if it's fiber cement board with screws underneath.

insights.title: Thermal Camera Limitations
insights.text: Thermal cameras often will not show wet spots under tile because the tile must be exposed to air for a temperature difference to appear. Rely on pinless readings, soft spots, and sound changed with knocking or tapping instead.
insights.conditions: Tile and hard surface flooring

voice_notes.tech: Thermal cameras are often useless under tile. Use pinless meter, feel for soft spots, and listen when you knock.
voice_notes.deep: Tile acts as a thermal barrier. Temperature differentials require air exposure. Traditional methods are more reliable here.
voice_notes.smartass: Your fancy thermal cam is basically blind under tile. Go back to the old ways.
voice_notes.rookie: Don't rely only on the thermal camera under tile floors. Check with pinless and by feel/sound too.

insights.title: Category 3 Aggressive Demo
insights.text: In Category 3 losses that have been sitting for multiple days, expect more aggressive demo. Two days is often enough time for significant bacterial and mold growth behind tile and in wall cavities.
insights.conditions: Category 3 with dwell time of 48+ hours

voice_notes.tech: Category 3 sitting more than a couple days usually means more aggressive demo. Mold and bacteria move fast behind finishes.
voice_notes.deep: Extended dwell time in black water allows rapid microbial amplification in cavities. Default to wider removal and more containment.
voice_notes.smartass: If black water sat more than two days, assume the walls are trying to grow their own ecosystem.
voice_notes.rookie: Black water that's been there a while usually needs more demo than it looks like at first.

insights.title: Extraction Before Containment
insights.text: Nobody in the real industry that I've seen have ever put up containments before extractions. They do put protection though, like floor protection and corner tape/covers.
insights.conditions: Most active or recent water losses

voice_notes.tech: Extract first. Contain after. Full plastic before extraction is usually the wrong order in real jobs.
voice_notes.deep: Primary goal is rapid water stoppage and limiting migration. Full containment setup is slower and messier once surfaces are wet.
voice_notes.smartass: Stop the bleeding before you build the operating room.
voice_notes.rookie: Get the water out first. Then build proper containment.

insights.title: Stop Water First
insights.text: Real-world reason extraction happens before containment: Primary goal is to stop the spread of water as fast as possible. Setting up full containment (plastic, tools, tape) is also much more difficult and messy once everything is already wet.
insights.conditions: Active water intrusion or recent loss

voice_notes.tech: Stop the water spread first. Full containment takes time and is harder to do cleanly once everything is wet.
voice_notes.deep: Containment is secondary to source control and rapid extraction. Wet conditions make proper sealing more difficult and time-consuming.
voice_notes.smartass: You can't contain a river. Stop the flow, then build the walls.
voice_notes.rookie: Get the water stopped and extracted before you spend time building full containment.

insights.title: Post Extraction Containment
insights.text: After extraction, containments are built to isolate the COL air volume.
insights.conditions: After initial extraction on water losses

voice_notes.tech: Build containment after extraction to isolate the affected air volume.
voice_notes.deep: Post-extraction containment prevents cross-contamination and controls the drying environment.
voice_notes.smartass: Now that you've stopped the flood, build the walls around what's left.
voice_notes.rookie: Once the water is out, set up containment to keep the bad air in one place.

insights.title: Framing Laser Taping
insights.text: For extra straight tape lines with lightning speed, use a framing laser.
insights.conditions: Setting up poly containment

voice_notes.tech: Use a framing laser for fast, straight tape lines when hanging poly.
voice_notes.deep: Laser levels dramatically speed up containment setup while improving quality and professionalism.
voice_notes.smartass: Your tape lines will look like you actually give a shit if you use a laser.
voice_notes.rookie: A cheap laser level makes containment go much faster and look way better.

insights.title: 80 Percent Circuit
insights.text: You can use 80% of an electrical circuit. A typical one is 10-15 amps. You can't always tell what's on which breaker. Sometimes breakers are mislabeled.
insights.conditions: When loading multiple pieces of equipment on one circuit

voice_notes.tech: Plan on 80% of the circuit rating. 15A breaker = ~12A continuous. Verify with load test when in doubt.
voice_notes.deep: NEC continuous load rule is 80%. Account for startup surges on dehus and always have a backup circuit plan.
voice_notes.smartass: Breakers lie and labels lie. Load test it or you're rolling the dice on a 2 a.m. trip.
voice_notes.rookie: Don't load more than about 80% of what the breaker is rated for.

insights.title: Purge Dehus Load Test
insights.text: If you have a lot of equipment setup, you can purge all the dehus at once to test the circuit under load.
insights.conditions: Large equipment setups with multiple dehumidifiers

voice_notes.tech: Purge all the dehus at once to load-test the circuit before you leave the job.
voice_notes.deep: Simultaneous startup creates the highest load. Testing under real conditions prevents mid-job breaker trips.
voice_notes.smartass: Turn them all on at once and see if the house fights back.
voice_notes.rookie: Start all your dehus together to make sure the circuit can actually handle it.

insights.title: Lipped Mat Dehus
insights.text: It's a good practice to place a lipped mat or tray under dehumidifiers in case there is a leak.
insights.conditions: Any dehumidifier setup, especially on finished floors

voice_notes.tech: Put a lipped mat or tray under dehus. Catches leaks before they become a bigger problem.
voice_notes.deep: Dehumidifiers can develop drain or coil leaks. Secondary containment prevents secondary damage claims.
voice_notes.smartass: Nothing says 'professional' like explaining why there's now water damage under your drying equipment.
voice_notes.rookie: Always put something under the dehu to catch leaks. It's cheap insurance.

insights.title: Boot Covers First
insights.text: Pro tip: when wearing a suit, slipping on boot covers first lets your feet glide in and out with less friction.
insights.conditions: When suiting up in PPE

voice_notes.tech: Put boot covers on first when wearing a suit. Much easier to get in and out.
voice_notes.deep: Reduces friction and wear on the suit and makes doffing cleaner and faster.
voice_notes.smartass: Trust me, fighting with boot covers while already in the suit is a special kind of hell.
voice_notes.rookie: Boot covers go on before you step into the suit. Makes life easier.

insights.title: Lath Plaster Demo
insights.text: Lath and plaster ceilings are difficult to demo, especially when conduit and plumbing are mounted on the finished side. Expect to use a carbide blade multi-tool. Sometimes fastened with screws, sometimes nails, and often has chicken wire behind it.
insights.conditions: Older homes with lath and plaster ceilings

voice_notes.tech: Lath and plaster is nasty. Carbide multi-tool blade, expect screws or nails, and often chicken wire behind it.
voice_notes.deep: Conduit and plumbing on the finished side makes clean removal difficult. Plan for more time and more dust control.
voice_notes.smartass: Old plaster likes to fight back. Bring the right blade and expect surprises.
voice_notes.rookie: Lath and plaster takes longer and makes more mess than drywall. Plan accordingly.

insights.title: Wood Paneling Cavities
insights.text: Wood paneling in basements is often installed over furring strips, creating hidden cavities that must be addressed in Category 3 losses.
insights.conditions: Basement wood paneling in Category 3 losses

voice_notes.tech: Wood paneling in basements usually has furring strips behind it. Those cavities need attention in cat 3.
voice_notes.deep: Furring creates dead air spaces that trap contamination. Full removal or treatment of cavities is often required.
voice_notes.smartass: That nice wood paneling is probably hiding a mold farm in the furring strips.
voice_notes.rookie: Check behind paneling — there are usually gaps that can hold water and mold.

insights.title: Minimize Contents Moves
insights.text: Be mindful of where you place things; the less you have to move them the better so plan ahead. For example, contents storage rooms, if demo'd, should be completely done and clean before contents are moved in, preventing the need for another hauling.
insights.conditions: Jobs requiring contents manipulation and storage

voice_notes.tech: Plan your moves. Finish and clean storage areas before bringing contents in so you don't have to move them twice.
voice_notes.deep: Every extra handling increases damage risk and labor cost. Sequence demo and cleaning to minimize touches.
voice_notes.smartass: Moving shit twice is how you turn a normal job into a long one.
voice_notes.rookie: Think ahead. Don't put contents in a room you're about to demo.

insights.title: Pressure Caps Lines
insights.text: Put pressure caps on opened water supply lines, even if the valve is secure. It looks professional and avoids liability.
insights.conditions: After shutting off or opening water supply lines

voice_notes.tech: Cap opened supply lines even if the valve is off. Looks professional and prevents surprises.
voice_notes.deep: Valves can fail or be bumped. Pressure caps are cheap insurance against secondary flooding.
voice_notes.smartass: Nothing says 'I care' like capping the line so it doesn't decide to flood again at 3 a.m.
voice_notes.rookie: Always cap open water lines. It's quick and prevents liability.

insights.title: Cut Higher Category 3
insights.text: In Category 3 losses with long dwell time (multiple days), treat affected walls and ceilings more aggressively. Cut higher than visible damage (typically 12–18 inches minimum on walls).
insights.conditions: Category 3 with extended dwell time

voice_notes.tech: Category 3 with time on it — cut higher than the visible line. 12-18 inches minimum on walls is common.
voice_notes.deep: Microbial growth extends beyond visible staining in black water with dwell time. Wider removal reduces risk of recurrence.
voice_notes.smartass: If it sat in cat 3, the mold didn't stop at the water line. Cut higher.
voice_notes.rookie: On old black water jobs, remove more than just the obviously damaged area.

insights.title: Sort Before Packing
insights.text: Sort on-site first before packing. Create three piles: Trash, Pack-out (worth sending to contents facility), and Clean on-site.
insights.conditions: Contents-heavy jobs

voice_notes.tech: Sort first on site: Trash, Pack-out, and Clean-on-site. Saves time and money downstream.
voice_notes.deep: Early triage reduces unnecessary handling and storage costs. Many items can be cleaned on site or discarded.
voice_notes.smartass: Sort it now or pay to move and store garbage later.
voice_notes.rookie: Don't pack everything. Sort into trash, pack-out, and clean-on-site piles first.

insights.title: Least To Most Damaged
insights.text: Work from least damaged areas to most damaged areas to avoid re-contaminating clean items.
insights.conditions: Contents manipulation on contaminated jobs

voice_notes.tech: Work clean to dirty. Start with the least damaged items so you don't contaminate them.
voice_notes.deep: Cross-contamination is easy when moving contents. Sequencing from cleanest to most affected protects salvageable items.
voice_notes.smartass: Don't drag clean stuff through the nasty stuff. Work smart.
voice_notes.rookie: Handle the cleaner items first so they stay clean.

insights.title: Aggressive Porous Trash
insights.text: Be aggressive with trash on heavily contaminated porous items (fabric, particle board, insulation, etc.), especially after long dwell times. It is often cheaper and faster than trying to clean them.
insights.conditions: Heavily contaminated porous materials with long dwell time

voice_notes.tech: On bad porous stuff with time on it, trash it. Cleaning is often more expensive than replacement.
voice_notes.deep: Porous materials with extended contamination exposure frequently fail cleaning validation. Economic decision favors replacement.
voice_notes.smartass: Some things are just expensive sponges at this point. Throw them away.
voice_notes.rookie: If it's really gross and porous and has been sitting, it's usually cheaper to replace it.

insights.title: Photograph Before Moving
insights.text: Photograph everything before moving it, especially high-value items.
insights.conditions: Any contents movement on a job

voice_notes.tech: Photo document before you move anything, especially high-value pieces. Protects everyone.
voice_notes.deep: Pre-move documentation reduces disputes and supports insurance claims. High-value items deserve extra attention.
voice_notes.smartass: Pictures save asses. Take them before you touch it.
voice_notes.rookie: Always take photos of contents before moving them. It's basic protection.

insights.title: Clean Staging Areas
insights.text: Use clean staging areas with poly or ram board. Never set dirty/sooty items directly on clean surfaces.
insights.conditions: Contents handling on fire or contaminated jobs

voice_notes.tech: Stage on clean poly or ram board. Never put dirty items directly on clean surfaces.
voice_notes.deep: Cross-contamination happens fast. Proper staging protects both contents and the structure.
voice_notes.smartass: Don't turn your clean staging area into another dirty zone.
voice_notes.rookie: Keep staging areas clean. Use poly or ram board so dirty items don't touch clean ones.

insights.title: Protein Natural Fire
insights.text: Protein fires (kitchen/grease) produce sticky, yellowish, hard-to-clean smoke. Natural fires (wood/paper) produce drier, powdery soot that is easier to clean.
insights.conditions: Fire damage assessment

voice_notes.tech: Protein (grease) fires = sticky yellow smoke. Natural fires = dry powdery soot. Changes cleaning approach.
voice_notes.deep: Protein residue is acidic and tenacious. Natural soot is more friable. Different chemistry requires different cleaning methods.
voice_notes.smartass: Kitchen fire? Expect the smoke to fight back. Wood fire? It's usually more cooperative.
voice_notes.rookie: Grease fires make nastier smoke than wood fires. Plan your cleaning method accordingly.

insights.title: Fire Job Demo Items
insights.text: What usually gets demo'd on fire jobs: carpet/pad, insulation, heavily affected drywall, particle board cabinets (especially on protein fires), and sometimes HVAC ductwork.
insights.conditions: Fire damage jobs

voice_notes.tech: Common demo on fire jobs: carpet/pad, insulation, bad drywall, particle board cabinets (especially protein fires), and sometimes ductwork.
voice_notes.deep: Porous and semi-porous materials in the smoke path are frequently non-salvageable. HVAC can become a distribution system.
voice_notes.smartass: If it burned or sat in heavy smoke, it's probably coming out.
voice_notes.rookie: Expect to remove carpet, pad, insulation, and heavily smoked drywall on most fire jobs.

insights.title: Full Gut Overkill
insights.text: Full gutting a house floor-to-ceiling just for smoke damage is usually overkill unless there is a very long dwell time (multiple weeks) combined with the fire getting into the HVAC system and actively pumping smoke throughout the house.
insights.conditions: Smoke damage without direct fire involvement

voice_notes.tech: Full gut for smoke is usually overkill unless it sat for weeks and went through the HVAC hard.
voice_notes.deep: Most smoke jobs can be cleaned and sealed without total demolition. Long dwell + HVAC distribution changes the equation.
voice_notes.smartass: You don't need to gut the whole house because someone burned toast three weeks ago.
voice_notes.rookie: Most smoke damage doesn't require a complete gut. Only extreme cases with long time and HVAC circulation do.

insights.title: Three Weeks HVAC Smoke
insights.text: Three weeks of smoke sitting in a house, especially if it circulated through the HVAC, significantly increases the likelihood that porous materials and drywall will need to be removed rather than cleaned and sealed.
insights.conditions: Smoke damage with extended dwell time and HVAC involvement

voice_notes.tech: Three weeks of smoke, especially through the HVAC, often means porous materials and drywall have to come out.
voice_notes.deep: Extended smoke residence time plus forced air distribution drives deep penetration into porous substrates. Cleaning success drops significantly.
voice_notes.smartass: If smoke lived in the house for three weeks and rode the HVAC, the walls are probably stained for life.
voice_notes.rookie: Long-term smoke with HVAC circulation usually means more demo than a fresh smoke job.
